concept.title: DTD Element Declarations
concept.shortdesc: Element declarations in a DTD define which elements can appear in a document and specify their allowed content — child elements, text, or a combination of both.

section.title: Element declaration syntax
section.p: The basic syntax for declaring an element is:
The content model specifies what the element can contain. There are several types:

section.title: Text-only elements (#PCDATA)
section.p: An element that contains only text (no child elements) uses #PCDATA (Parsed Character Data):
section.note: #PCDATA stands for "Parsed Character Data" — meaning the parser will process entity references like & inside the text. It does not mean the element can only contain plain text strings.

section.title: Elements with child elements (sequences)
section.p: An element that must contain specific child elements in a specific order uses a comma-separated list:
This means a <book> must contain exactly one <title>, followed by one <author>, then one <year>, then one <price> — in that exact order.

section.title: Occurrence indicators: How many times?
section.p: By default, a child element must appear exactly once. You can change this with occurrence indicators:

row.entry: author* — any number of authors (including none)
One or more (at least one required)
chapter+ — at least one chapter required

section.codeblock: <!-- A book has one title, optional subtitle, one or more authors, and one price --> <!ELEMENT book (title, subtitle?, author+, price)>

section.title: Choice groups (alternatives)
section.p: Use the pipe character (|) to indicate that one of several elements can appear:
section.codeblock: <!-- A payment is EITHER a credit-card OR a bank-transfer OR cash --> <!ELEMENT payment (credit-card | bank-transfer | cash)>
section.p: You can combine sequences and choices with parentheses:
section.codeblock: <!-- A product has a name, then either a description or a summary, then a price --> <!ELEMENT product (name, (description | summary), price)>

section.title: Mixed content
section.p: An element with mixed content can contain both text and child elements. The syntax uses #PCDATA with choices and the * indicator:
section.note: In mixed content declarations, #PCDATA must be listed first, and the group must use choice (|) with *. You cannot use sequences with mixed content.

section.title: EMPTY and ANY content
section.codeblock:
<!-- Element must have no content --> <!ELEMENT linebreak EMPTY>
<!-- Element can contain anything (text, elements, or both) --> <!ELEMENT container ANY>
section.p: EMPTY is common for elements like line breaks or images that carry information through attributes only. ANY is rarely used in production because it provides no validation.

section.title: Complete example: A bookstore DTD
section.codeblock: <!ELEMENT bookstore (book+)> <!ELEMENT book (title, author+, year, price, description?)> <!ELEMENT title (#PCDATA)> <!ELEMENT author (#PCDATA)> <!ELEMENT year (#PCDATA)> <!ELEMENT price (#PCDATA)> <!ELEMENT description (#PCDATA | bold | italic)*> <!ELEMENT bold (#PCDATA)> <!ELEMENT italic (#PCDATA)>

ul.li: A <bookstore> contains one or more <book> elements
A <book> has a required title, one or more authors, a year, a price, and an optional description
The <description> can contain mixed content with bold and italic markup
